data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "The collapse was driven by a systemic fragility in the palatial redistribution economy where hyper-specialization created a single point of failure; the interruption of critical tin imports via maritime routes caused a cascading loss of military and agricultural tool efficacy, rendering the states unable to suppress internal unrest or external raids.", "mechanism": "Supply chain cascade failure leading to institutional paralysis", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states"], "excludes": ["Egypt", "Cyprus", "Mesopotamia"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Archaeological evidence of abundant bronze stockpiles in destroyed layers post-1200 BC", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Synthesizes the bronze supply chain hypothesis with systemic fragility models, relying on the interdependence of the neighborhood artifacts regarding economic collapse."}
